University of Negros Occidental–Recoletos · College of Information Technology

Predicting Student Performance Rating
and Employability Class
from Mock Interviews

Linear Regression Logistic Regression Four Competencies Philippine Higher Education Machine Learning

A quantitative comparative study evaluating which predictive modeling approach—linear or logistic regression—yields stronger insights from mock interview competency scores for student employability analytics.

Leone Kidlat R. Sevillo leonesevillo93@gmail.com
Cean Mykell Kyne Canoy Ceanmykellkyne@gmail.com
O'Neil Miguel Iguidez oneilmigueliguidezmcu@gmail.com
2,982
Student Records
4
Competencies
2
ML Models
§ 01 — Abstract

Study Overview

Graduate employability has become a central concern in Philippine higher education as institutions are increasingly expected to demonstrate the practical value of academic programs. This study applies two supervised machine learning algorithms—multiple linear regression for continuous outcome prediction and binary logistic regression for classification—to mock interview competency scores from the Students' Employability Dataset – Philippines.

"Which statistical modeling approach provides a more effective and actionable framework for analyzing mock job interview data to enhance student employability support at the university level?"

Both models were trained on four standardized competency dimensions: Communication Skills, Self-Confidence, Ability to Present Ideas, and Mental Alertness. Linear Regression predicted the continuous Student Performance Rating, while Logistic Regression classified students as Employable or LessEmployable.

The linear model achieved an R² of 0.0668 and RMSE of 0.6993, indicating limited variance explained. The logistic model reached an accuracy of 55.95% with AUC-ROC of 0.5906, only marginally better than random chance. Despite weak individual performance, a hybrid workflow—using logistic regression for initial screening followed by linear regression for fine-grained ranking—is recommended for career services.

§ 02 — Methodology

Research Design & Methods

01

Data Source

Students' Employability Dataset – Philippines (Hamoutni, 2022) from Kaggle. 2,982 anonymized mock interview records from Philippine college students with 7 competency ratings.

02

Preprocessing

Missing values imputed using median. Features standardized (mean=0, SD=1) via StandardScaler. Four competencies retained; three excluded for low predictive contribution.

03

Train-Test Split

80/20 stratified split using random_state=42 for reproducibility. Training set used exclusively for model fitting; test set held-out for evaluation.

04

Linear Regression

OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) method. Predicts continuous Student Performance Rating (1–5 scale). Evaluated via R², RMSE, and MAE.

05

Logistic Regression

L2 regularization, max 1,000 iterations, threshold=0.5. Predicts binary Class (Employable=1, LessEmployable=0). Evaluated via Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC-ROC.

06

Implementation

Python 3.10 with scikit-learn, pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and seaborn. Full code reproducible with fixed random seed across all stochastic operations.

Feature Selection — Competency Dimensions

Mental Alertness
Self-Confidence
Ability to Present Ideas
Communication Skills
General Appearance
Manner of Speaking
Physical Condition

The three excluded dimensions showed negligible predictive contribution in preliminary analysis and were dropped to improve model parsimony.

Research Figures (click to enlarge)

Figure 1

Correlation Matrix of Competency Scores. Strongest correlations: Self-Confidence ↔ Mental Alertness (0.68), Communication Skills ↔ Ability to Present Ideas (0.65).

Figure 2

Distribution of Student Performance Rating and Employability Class. Ratings peak at 5; class split is ~52% Employable, ~48% LessEmployable.

Figure 3

Boxplots by employability class. Mental Alertness and Self-Confidence show clearer separation between classes.

Figure 4

Actual vs. Predicted Student Performance Rating. Vertical clustering reveals ordinal scale limitations and moderate agreement.

Figure 5

Residual plot for Linear Regression. Residuals roughly centered at zero, supporting homoscedasticity with structured patterns from discrete targets.

Figure 6

Confusion Matrix: 247 true positives, 87 true negatives, 99 false negatives, 164 false positives. High false positive rate noted.

Figure 7

ROC Curve: AUC = 0.5906 — only marginally above the random classifier baseline of 0.5, indicating weak discriminative ability.

§ 05 — Discussion & Conclusion

Findings & Recommendations

Linear Regression Findings

Explained only 6.7% of variance in Student Performance Rating (R² = 0.0668, RMSE = 0.6993). The near-zero R² suggests evaluator ratings are largely independent of these four competency scores, or that the rating scale suffers from range restriction (most scores are 4 or 5).

Logistic Regression Findings

Achieved 55.95% accuracy and AUC of 0.5906—only marginally above random chance. The model correctly identified 71% of employable students but generated many false positives (precision only 60%).

Competency Drivers

The two models disagreed on rankings. Linear regression identified Ability to Present Ideas highest; logistic regression ranked Mental Alertness first. Both suggest Communication Skills and Mental Alertness as relatively more influential.

Practical Recommendation

A hybrid workflow is recommended: use logistic regression for initial binary screening, then apply linear regression to rank flagged students. Richer features (GPA, internships, socioeconomic data) and actual job placement targets are needed for operational deployment.

Future Directions

Future work should incorporate longitudinal employment data, broader contextual variables, and non-linear ensemble methods (random forests, gradient boosting). Cross-institutional validation is recommended to improve generalizability.

SDG Alignment

The study contributes to SDG 4 (Quality Education) and SDG 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth) by providing an evidence-based framework for strengthening the transition from education to employment in the Philippine higher education context.
